title: "podman pull" ai models like "docker pull" can
body:
### Feature request description

docker has pushable/pullable llms:

```
$ docker pull docker.io/ai/smollm2
Using default tag: latest
latest: Pulling from ai/smollm2
^Ccontext canceled
$ podman pull docker.io/ai/smollm2
Trying to pull docker.io/ai/smollm2:latest...
Error: parsing image configuration: unsupported image-specific operation on artifact with type "application/vnd.docker.ai.model.config.v0.1+json"
```

these aren't pullable with podman.

Also might be worth adding Ollama pulls, they are 4 layer OCI images that are pulled.



### Suggest potential solution

A clear and concise description of what you want to happen.

### Have you considered any alternatives?

A clear and concise description of any alternative solutions or features you've considered.

### Additional context

Add any other context or screenshots about the feature request here.

comments.body: @rhatdan I tried this, works with a plain-old "docker pull" but not a plain-old "podman pull" because it's new

Hi @ericcurtin, @baude has added initial support for handling OCI Artifact images into Podman last year.

If you are running a recent version of Podman you can run `podman artifact pull docker.io/ai/smollm2` and see it in action! 🚀 

So, I wonder if you are asking if we can mimic the behavior of docker by adding support for `podman pull` to detect the user is attempting to pull an OCI Artifact image and route it though `podman artifact pull` transparently for the end user? 
 
We are planning to expand this feature this quarter so please let us know so we can consider it!

Hi @ninja-quokka are we sure @baude implemented "application/vnd.docker.ai.model.config.v0.1+json" image type before it was even released by docker? If yes that's really impressive.

I'm aware of podman artifact, waiting for that to percolate down to Fedora, seems super cool :)

@ericcurtin, `artifact pull` is agnostic to the specific artifact and does not interpret it.  Are you looking for `application/vnd.docker.ai.model.config.v0.1+json` to be interpreted as a model and be executed as such?

Can you link the actual documentation about the format and how that is actually consumed in docker? It is not clear to me that these are actually runnable images that should be able to be pulled via a normal podman pull.

It's defined here:

https://github.com/docker/model-distribution/blob/main/pkg/types/config.go

even if it's not runnable. It's still useful to pull it into the container store, once we have this in the local container store we have access to the .gguf file, etc.

> [@ericcurtin](https://github.com/ericcurtin), `artifact pull` is agnostic to the specific artifact and does not interpret it. Are you looking for `application/vnd.docker.ai.model.config.v0.1+json` to be interpreted as a model and be executed as such?

To be interpreted as just another OCI image is fine, even if it's not runnable

> https://github.com/docker/model-distribution/blob/main/pkg/types/config.go

That is not documentation about the format, it is an implementation.

> even if it's not runnable. It's still useful to pull it into the container store, once we have this in the local container store we have access to the .gguf file, etc.

Not really, the container store is a layer store that expects a valid rootfs generally that can be mounted. That does not seem to be such thing.
We designed podman artifact exactly for these kind of things. You can already pull this as artifact and work with it as artifact.

On main, we already have `podman artifact extract` to get the data as files or even `--mount type=artifact,...` to mount it into a container as files. 

I don't think a podman pull should work on that if it is not an actual runnable image, how would you actually use that in docker?

Do you think it makes sense to document how to pull docker ai and ollama models somewhere?

Even still as a user, "podman artifact" is not so well known, I am spreading awareness, most when I tell them have no idea it exists or is in an upcoming release.

I think from the UX perspective, it might make sense to further enhance to align with docker, I mean it doesn't get simpler than:

`docker pull docker.io/ai/smollm2`

> > https://github.com/docker/model-distribution/blob/main/pkg/types/config.go
> 
> That is not documentation about the format, it is an implementation.
> 
> > even if it's not runnable. It's still useful to pull it into the container store, once we have this in the local container store we have access to the .gguf file, etc.
> 
> Not really, the container store is a layer store that expects a valid rootfs generally that can be mounted. That does not seem to be such thing. We designed podman artifact exactly for these kind of things. You can already pull this as artifact and work with it as artifact.
> 
> On main, we already have `podman artifact extract` to get the data as files or even `--mount type=artifact,...` to mount it into a container as files.
> 
> I don't think a podman pull should work on that if it is not an actual runnable image, how would you actually use that in docker?

In docker it's used via another command "docker model run".

I guess an equivalent tool right now would to make these type of images work with "ramalama run" since there is no "podman model run"

> I think from the UX perspective, it might make sense to further enhance to align with docker, I mean it doesn't get simpler than:
> 
> `docker pull docker.io/ai/smollm2`

I do get that compatibility is worth to consider. But at least to me this violates POLA.

If you can do podman pull x, but then `podman run` being unable to actually run it that seems very bad design to me. It also will not act/behave like a normal image. Even if we would patch `podman images` to show these you would still have a lot of compatibility issues with commands like podman image inspect/mount/unmount/diff and many more.

> > I think from the UX perspective, it might make sense to further enhance to align with docker, I mean it doesn't get simpler than:
> > `docker pull docker.io/ai/smollm2`
> 
> I do get that compatibility is worth to consider. But at least to me this violates POLA.
> 
> If you can do podman pull x, but then `podman run` being unable to actually run it that seems very bad design to me. It also will not act/behave like a normal image. Even if we would patch `podman images` to show these you would still have a lot of compatibility issues with commands like podman image inspect/mount/unmount/diff and many more.

We could do a spike on how docker does this

I don't think it's terrible UX if we pull an image and with "podman run" we error out with something along the lines of:

`podman cannot run ai images`

unrunnable images isn't a new thing, I've downloaded images of the wrong cpu architecture before that have been unrunnable.

https://docs.docker.com/desktop/features/model-runner/

is the only thing I can find which somewhat explains what this is doing but even that says `docker model pull` not `docker pull`?.

> For: Docker Desktop for Mac with Apple Silicon

And that is implemented as cli plugin (so not actually part of docker, like compose) and I guess it is not open source either.

---

> I don't think it's terrible UX if we pull an image and with "podman run" we error out with something along the lines of:
>
> podman cannot run ai images
>
> unrunnable images isn't a new thing, I've downloaded images of the wrong cpu architecture before that have been unrunnable.

Well unrunnable sure, but that still had a proper rootfs that could be mounted, inspected, diff'ed, etc... And you could setup emulation to run it as well.
Making podman pull all of the sudden do something completely different to me causes much more design/functionality problems. podman artifact was exactly designed to handle these things so it should be used for such use cases.

> Hi [@ninja-quokka](https://github.com/ninja-quokka) are we sure [@baude](https://github.com/baude) implemented "application/vnd.docker.ai.model.config.v0.1+json" image type before it was even released by docker? If yes that's really impressive.
> 

I've only been in the team a few days but AFAIU @baude is more of a Wizard than a Time Lord ;)

My understanding is Docker is just using OCI artifacts under the hood of their "gen-ai catalog" and this is the part we have support for today. 

It's only an ["experimental and still in development" ](https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-artifact.1.html) feature at the moment and only supports the Linux client which is what we plan to fix this quarter. 

> I'm aware of podman artifact, waiting for that to percolate down to Fedora, seems super cool :)

You needn't wait any longer! I was testing it in my Fedora 41 VM so it's currently available in the repos :)

OCI Images do not have to be `runnable`.  The OCI IMage has been abused for years to store random stuff, including AI Models.   Different ARCH images usually are also not runable.  

I am thinking podman pull should either pull the image or at least throw out an error telling users to try with podman artifact pull.

Bottom line from the users perspective, they are not going to understand the difference when an OCI Image should be pulled versus an artifact.  Docker seems to be hiding the difference slightly.

The podman team should think about how they will participate in the emerging world of AI.

I am not 100% sure this is the correct thing to do, but it should definitely be discussed.
I smell some great blog posts on developers.redhat.com :^)

Also "podman artifact" might be capable of pulling Ollama models already, the final part of the Ollama protocol is basically an OCI pull:

https://github.com/containers/ramalama/issues/1112

When I say final part, the first part is just resolving a short name server side to a longer name

@ericcurtin On main (coming in podman 5.5) you can mount an artifact into the container.
```
$ podman run --mount type=artifact,src=docker.io/ai/smollm2:latest,dst=/model quay.io/libpod/testimage:20241011 ls /mode
l
sha256-bf6f20a603055433b4b998119e17928fc4a89b35c42855dd7eada105058cae0a
sha256-cfc7749b96f63bd31c3c42b5c471bf756814053e847c10f3eb003417bc523d30
```

The files names are derived from the digest, it normally defaults to using the name from the `org.opencontainers.image.title` annotation but of course the docker ai models don't seem to set that so we are left with the digest.

If you only want to mount a single file you can filter by digest
```
$ bin/podman run --mount type=artifact,src=docker.io/ai/smollm2:latest,dst=/model,digest=sha256:bf6f20a603055433b4b998119e1792
8fc4a89b35c42855dd7eada105058cae0a quay.io/libpod/testimage:20241011 ls /model/
sha256-bf6f20a603055433b4b998119e17928fc4a89b35c42855dd7eada105058cae0a
```

Now of course that overall gives you very little control over the final file name in the image when the org.opencontainers.image.title is missing and you don't control how the artifact is created/don't want to modify it.

We are open to extend artifact mounting with further options if needed. And IMO if there are certains mediaType/artifactType's that are well defined I think we can consider special casing application/vnd.docker.ai.gguf.v3 to mount as model.gguf or something like that instead of defaulting to the digest name.

> [@ericcurtin](https://github.com/ericcurtin) On main (coming in podman 5.5) you can mount an artifact into the container.
> 
> ```
> $ podman run --mount type=artifact,src=docker.io/ai/smollm2:latest,dst=/model quay.io/libpod/testimage:20241011 ls /mode
> l
> sha256-bf6f20a603055433b4b998119e17928fc4a89b35c42855dd7eada105058cae0a
> sha256-cfc7749b96f63bd31c3c42b5c471bf756814053e847c10f3eb003417bc523d30
> ```
> 
> The files names are derived from the digest, it normally defaults to using the name from the `org.opencontainers.image.title` annotation but of course the docker ai models don't seem to set that so we are left with the digest.
> 
> If you only want to mount a single file you can filter by digest
> 
> ```
> $ bin/podman run --mount type=artifact,src=docker.io/ai/smollm2:latest,dst=/model,digest=sha256:bf6f20a603055433b4b998119e1792
> 8fc4a89b35c42855dd7eada105058cae0a quay.io/libpod/testimage:20241011 ls /model/
> sha256-bf6f20a603055433b4b998119e17928fc4a89b35c42855dd7eada105058cae0a
> ```
> 
> Now of course that overall gives you very little control over the final file name in the image when the org.opencontainers.image.title is missing and you don't control how the artifact is created/don't want to modify it.
> 
> We are open to extend artifact mounting with further options if needed. And IMO if there are certains mediaType/artifactType's that are well defined I think we can consider special casing application/vnd.docker.ai.gguf.v3 to mount as model.gguf or something like that instead of defaulting to the digest name.

This would be interesting to try with an Ollama-type model too, to give you an ideal of what that type of artifact looks like:

https://github.com/ericcurtin/lm-pull/blob/f688fb83fce2c96efeadb096b6cdaea11e133d4a/lm-pull.py#L262

> @Luap99 while it would be nice to have a name, we should have a default based on the OCI Name perhaps? The base name?

The artifact has more than one blob that needs to be mounted, so the default must pick some name that is unique for each blob. That is why we default to using `org.opencontainers.image.title` as name which is added by podman artfifact add for example. Of course if external artifacts don't have it then we the only thing that is universally there will be the digest.

I agree that the naming is not great and we need better options to customize it, also artifact is marked as experimental currently so we are free to break the current behavior. I am certainly open for better suggestions there.

If we would pick the basename, then we need to add an index as suffix?! i.e. basename-1, basename-2, etc... when there is more than one blob.

>  But a standard would be great.

Sure but it is not for us (podman) to define that, is it?
The basename-1, basename-2 is a good idea, Buildah uses this for nameing containers.
The OCI would be the place to define the standard. But this is going to take a while. (Not something I am going to work on).

Sometimes it's worth just following the informal standard... QUIC won the http3 battle before they considered standardizing... OpenAI protocol is an effective standard for AI communication even though I'm not sure any group formally standardized it...

> The OCI would be the place to define the standard. But this is going to take a while. (Not something I am going to work on).

Aren't OCI Artifacts defined outside the OCI spec?   Agreeing on a "model artifact" would probably happen outside the OCI scope.

I think it's an exciting time at the moment with the different approaches somehow competing.  Once the dust has settled, it should be easier to standardize on one spec.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

With https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/26430 the situation should be better.

So with that the flow can be
```
podman artifact pull docker.io/ai/smollm2:latest

# inspect to get digest for media type
podman artifact inspect docker.io/ai/smollm2:latest | jq -r ' .Manifest.layers[] | select(.mediaType=="application/vnd.docker.
ai.gguf.v3").digest '

podman run --rm --mount type=artifact,src=docker.io/ai/smollm2:latest,dst=/mnt/model/model.file,digest=$digest $IMAGE
```

Also there now is a name=xxx option on the mount in case you link to mount an artifact with multiple blobs and specify a more sane name.

I think that is enough to enable most use cases. Of course one could still discuss whenever or not podman should learn how to interpret certain media types directly and have special handling for those.
